translation: to go / to come / to be (sonkeigo)
naturalMeaning: The single sonkeigo verb that covers 行く, 来る, and いる — which one it means is read from context. Elevates the subject, so you use it for a customer, client, or superior, never for yourself.

examples.text: 社長は明日いらっしゃいます。
examples.reading: しゃちょうはあしたいらっしゃいます。
examples.translation: The president will be here/go/come tomorrow.

naturalMeaning: The sonkeigo replacement for する — irregular ます-stem なさいます, not なさります.

examples.text: お客様は何をなさいますか。
examples.reading: おきゃくさまはなにをなさいますか。
examples.romaji: Okyakusama wa nani o nasaimasu ka.

naturalMeaning: The sonkeigo replacement for 言う — irregular ます-stem おっしゃいます, not おっしゃります.

examples.text: 部長がそうおっしゃいました。
examples.reading: ぶちょうがそうおっしゃいました。

naturalMeaning: The sonkeigo replacement for 見る — built from an お/ご + noun + になる pattern, but memorized as a set phrase since 覧 alone isn't used this way elsewhere.

examples.text: こちらの資料をご覧になりましたか。
examples.reading: こちらのしりょうをごらんになりましたか。
examples.romaji: Kochira no shiryō o goran ni narimashita ka.
examples.translation: Have you looked at this document?

naturalMeaning: The kenjougo replacement for 行く/来る — lowers the speaker, so it's used for your own movement, never someone else's.

examples.text: 明日十時にまいります。
examples.reading: あしたじゅうじにまいります。

translation: to visit / to ask / to hear (kenjougo)
naturalMeaning: A three-way kenjougo verb: covers 行く/来る (visit), 聞く (ask), and 聞く (hear/be told) — which meaning applies comes from context.

examples.text: 一つ伺ってもよろしいでしょうか。
examples.reading: ひとつうかがってもよろしいでしょうか。
examples.translation: May I ask you one thing?

naturalMeaning: The kenjougo replacement for する — the most common kenjougo verb in business Japanese, seen constantly in set phrases like よろしくお願いいたします.

examples.reading: わたしがたんとういたします。
examples.translation: I will be the one in charge.

naturalMeaning: The plain kenjougo replacement for 言う, best known from the self-introduction line ～と申します.
A deeper kenjougo version of 申す, built from 申す + 上げる — used toward someone clearly above you, common in お礼申し上げます ("I express my thanks").

examples.text: 心よりお礼申し上げます。
examples.reading: こころよりおれいもうしあげます。

naturalMeaning: The kenjougo replacement for 見る — lowers your own act of looking, common when reviewing a client's documents.

examples.reading: しりょうをはいけんしました。
examples.translation: I have looked over the documents.

translation: to receive / to eat / to drink (kenjougo)
naturalMeaning: Kenjougo for もらう (receive) — also doubles as the humble form of 食べる/飲む. Also the base of the request pattern ～ていただけますか.

examples.text: 資料をいただきました。
examples.reading: しりょうをいただきました。

translation: to give (kenjougo, to someone above you)
naturalMeaning: Kenjougo for あげる — lowers the giver (you) when giving to someone of higher status; overused toward equals it can sound obsequious.

examples.text: 資料をお送り差し上げます。
examples.reading: しりょうをおおくりさしあげます。
examples.translation: I will send you the documents.

translation: to give (sonkeigo, someone above gives to you)
naturalMeaning: Sonkeigo for くれる — elevates the giver when they give something to you or your in-group; irregular ます-stem くださいます.

examples.text: 部長が資料をくださいました。
examples.reading: ぶちょうがしりょうをくださいました。
examples.translation: The department head gave me the documents.

naturalMeaning: Kenjougo for 知る/思う — common in the set phrase 存じております ("I am aware").

examples.text: その件は存じております。
examples.reading: そのけんはぞんじております。
examples.translation: I am aware of that matter.

naturalMeaning: The sonkeigo counterpart to 存じる — asks whether the listener knows something, as in ご存知ですか ("Are you aware...?"). Easy to confuse with the kenjougo 存じております, which describes the speaker's own knowledge instead.

examples.text: 田中部長をご存知ですか。
examples.reading: たなかぶちょうをごぞんじですか。
examples.romaji: Tanaka buchō o gozonji desu ka.
examples.translation: Do you know Department Head Tanaka?

examples.text: 名刺を交換しましょう。
examples.reading: めいしをこうかんしましょう。
かいぎはさんじからです。

naturalMeaning: The standard business/service way to refer to a customer or client — お客 alone is plainer, さん is too casual for service contexts.
A working meeting to align on details — smaller and less formal than 会議, common between two people or small teams.

lemma: お世話になっております
reading: おせわになっております
translation: Thank you for your continued support
naturalMeaning: The standard opening line of a business phone call or email to someone at a partner company — literally "I am indebted to your care," said regardless of whether anything specific happened recently.

lemma: よろしくお願いいたします
reading: よろしくおねがいいたします
translation: Thank you in advance / I appreciate your cooperation
naturalMeaning: The kenjougo-upgraded version of よろしくお願いします (いたす replacing する) — used to close emails, requests, and introductions in business contexts.

reading: もうしわけございません
naturalMeaning: A formal apology, stronger than すみません — ございません is the polite negative of ござる, keigo's formal copula. Standard for apologizing to a client for a delay or mistake.
A flexible business phrase used entering someone's office, stepping out of a meeting, or hanging up a phone call — literally "I will be rude."

reading: しょうしょうおまちください
naturalMeaning: The formal service-register version of ちょっと待ってください — standard at reception desks and on business phone calls.
The formal service-register version of わかりました — signals full compliance, common from staff toward customers or from a subordinate toward a superior.

reading: おさきにしつれいします
naturalMeaning: Said when leaving the office before coworkers who are still working — a small courtesy phrase, not a literal apology.

translation: Thank you for your hard work (greeting)
naturalMeaning: An all-purpose workplace greeting used at almost any time of day among coworkers, regardless of whether anyone is actually tired — the past-tense お疲れ様でした is used when work is finished.

translation: I will be waiting for you / looking forward to it
naturalMeaning: A kenjougo pattern (お + stem + する, then ～ております for the humble ongoing form) used to close an email or invitation politely.

translation: Could you possibly do ~ for me?
naturalMeaning: The polite-request pattern built on いただく — softer and more deferential than ～てください, standard when asking a superior or client for something.

examples.text: 少しお待ちいただけますか。
examples.reading: すこしおまちいただけますか。
examples.translation: Could you please wait a little?
